label: Determinant
definition: A single number computed from a square matrix that measures the signed volume scaling of the linear transformation it represents, zero exactly when the matrix is singular, and a recurring factor in change-of-variables formulas and probabilistic models.

body.text: The determinant is a single scalar distilled from a square matrix that captures, in one number, how the [linear transformation](linear-transformation) represented by that matrix changes volume. Apply the matrix to a unit cube and it maps to a parallelepiped; the determinant is the signed volume of that image. A determinant of three means the transformation triples volumes, a determinant of one half means it shrinks them by half, and a negative determinant means the transformation also flips orientation, turning a right-handed frame into a left-handed one. This geometric reading, volume scaling with a sign for orientation, is the single most useful way to understand what the determinant is.

The most important fact about the determinant is when it equals zero. A determinant of zero means the transformation collapses space into a lower dimension, squashing volume to nothing, which happens exactly when the [matrix](matrix) is singular: its columns are linearly dependent, its rank is deficient, and it has no inverse. So the determinant is a direct test of invertibility, and a matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is nonzero. A determinant close to zero signals that the matrix is nearly singular and that inverting it will be numerically unstable, amplifying small errors enormously.

The determinant is tightly bound to the rest of linear algebra. It equals the product of the matrix's [eigenvalues](eigenvalue), which makes intuitive sense because eigenvalues are the stretch factors along the eigen-directions and volume scaling is their product. It is also the product of the squared singular values' relationship to volume, connecting it to the [singular value decomposition](singular-value-decomposition). It is multiplicative, the determinant of a product is the product of the determinants, and the determinant of an inverse is the reciprocal. These properties let it serve as a compact summary of how a transformation distorts space.

In machine learning the determinant appears most prominently through the change-of-variables formula, which tells you how a probability density transforms when its variable is pushed through an invertible map: the new density is the old one divided by the absolute value of the determinant of the map's [Jacobian](jacobian). This is the mathematical core of normalizing flows, which build flexible distributions by composing invertible transformations and must compute log-determinants efficiently to evaluate likelihood. The log-determinant of a covariance matrix appears in the log-density of a multivariate [Gaussian distribution](gaussian-distribution) and in the objective of Gaussian processes. Because naive determinant computation is expensive and unstable, practitioners work with log-determinants and exploit structure such as triangular or low-rank forms to make these terms tractable.

image.alt: A parallelogram in the plane with one corner at the origin, spanned by the vectors (a, b) and (c, d). Its fourth corner is at (a plus c, b plus d), and its interior is labeled with the area ad minus bc.
image.caption: The determinant of a two-by-two matrix is the signed area of the parallelogram its columns span, here ad minus bc. So it measures how much the transformation scales area (volume in higher dimensions): a determinant of zero means the parallelogram collapses to a line, which is exactly when the matrix is singular and has no inverse.
